7.1: Jerubbaal—the man they called Gideon—rose at dawn with all his men and made camp beside the spring of Harod. The Midianite horde lay to the north, at the foot of the hill of Moreh, down in the valley below.
7.2: The LORD said to Gideon, “You have too many men. If I hand Midian over to them now, Israel will take the credit and say, 'We won this by our own strength'—and I will not share my glory with human pride.”
7.3: So Gideon made the announcement: “If any man here is frightened or anxious, he may go home from Mount Gilead right now.” Twenty-two thousand walked away. Only ten thousand remained.
7.4: Still the LORD said, “There are too many. Bring them down to the water and I will winnow them there. I will tell you who goes and who turns back.”
7.5: Gideon brought the men to the water. The LORD said: “Separate those who lap up water with their tongues like a dog from those who kneel down to drink.”
7.6: Three hundred men lapped water by scooping it up to their mouths with their hands. All the rest knelt down to drink.
7.7: The LORD said to Gideon, “These three hundred who lapped—with them I will deliver you, and I will give Midian into your hands. Send all the others home.”
7.8: The three hundred men took the food and the trumpets of those who left. Gideon dismissed the rest of Israel to their tents and kept only his three hundred. Down in the valley the Midianite camp spread out before them.
7.9: That night the LORD spoke again: “Get up and go down to the camp. I have given it into your hand.”
7.10: But if fear holds you back, take Purah your servant and go down to the camp together.
7.11: Listen to what they are saying, and it will give you the courage you need.“ So Gideon slipped down with Purah to the edge of the enemy camp, where the sentinels stood.
7.12: The Midianites, Amalekites, and all the eastern peoples blanketed the valley like a swarm of locusts. Their camels were beyond counting—as many as grains of sand on the shore.
7.13: As Gideon arrived, he heard one soldier telling another about a dream: “I dreamed that a loaf of barley bread went rolling down into our camp. It struck a tent and the whole thing collapsed flat.”
7.14: His companion answered, “That can only mean one thing—the sword of Gideon son of Joash, the Israelite. God has handed Midian and this whole army over to him.”
7.15: The moment Gideon heard the dream and its meaning, he bowed and worshiped. Then he hurried back to the Israelite camp and called out, “On your feet! The LORD has given the Midianite army into our hands!”
7.16: He split his three hundred men into three groups, giving each man a trumpet and an empty clay jar with a torch hidden inside.
7.17: He told them, “Watch me and follow my lead exactly. When I reach the edge of the camp, do whatever I do.”
7.18: The moment I blow my trumpet—and all my men blow theirs—you blow yours from every side of the camp and shout: 'The sword of the LORD and of Gideon!'
7.19: Gideon and his hundred men reached the edge of the camp at the start of the middle watch—just when the sentries had been changed. All at once they blew their trumpets and shattered the jars.
7.20: All three groups blew their trumpets and smashed their jars simultaneously. Torches blazed in their left hands, trumpets roared from their right, and they all cried out: “The sword of the LORD and of Gideon!”
7.21: Every man held his position all around the camp. Inside, panic broke out—the Midianites screamed and fled in every direction.
7.22: As the three hundred trumpets rang out, the LORD turned the Midianite soldiers against each other—swords flashing in their own camp. The survivors fled all the way to Beth-shittah and Zererah, on to the border of Abel-meholah near Tabbath.
7.23: Warriors from Naphtali, Asher, and all Manasseh rallied and joined the pursuit of the fleeing Midianites.
7.24: Gideon sent messengers throughout the Ephraimite highlands: “Come down! Cut off the Midianites at the fords—hold the waters of Beth-barah and the Jordan against them!” The men of Ephraim mustered and seized those crossing points.
7.25: They captured the two Midianite warlords, Oreb and Zeeb—killing Oreb at the rock that now bears his name, and Zeeb at the winepress that bears his. They pressed on after the Midianite army and brought the heads of Oreb and Zeeb to Gideon across the Jordan.

8.1: The men of Ephraim confronted Gideon angrily: “Why didn't you call us when you went to fight Midian? What kind of slight is this?” They were furious.
8.2: Gideon answered them gently: “What have I done compared to you? Even the leftover grapes of Ephraim are better than the entire harvest of Abiezer.”
8.3: God gave you the Midianite princes—Oreb and Zeeb! What I did is nothing beside that.“ At these words their anger cooled.
8.4: Gideon reached the Jordan and crossed it with his three hundred men—every one of them spent and hungry, but still pressing forward.
8.5: He asked the people of Succoth, “Give my men some bread—they are exhausted. I am still chasing Zebah and Zalmunna, the kings of Midian.”
8.6: The leaders of Succoth refused: “Have you already caught Zebah and Zalmunna? Why should we feed an army that hasn't won yet?”
8.7: Gideon replied, “So be it. When the LORD delivers Zebah and Zalmunna into my hand, I will come back and thresh your flesh with desert thorns and briers.”
8.8: He went on to Penuel and made the same request. The people of Penuel gave him the same cold refusal as Succoth.
8.9: To the men of Penuel he said, “When I come back victorious, I will pull this tower down.”
8.10: Zebah and Zalmunna had regrouped at Karkor with about fifteen thousand men—the remnant of the eastern armies. One hundred and twenty thousand had already fallen in battle.
8.11: Gideon came at them by the nomads' road east of Nobah and Jogbehah and struck the army when they were least expecting it.
8.12: Zebah and Zalmunna tried to flee, but Gideon ran them down and captured both kings. The entire army was thrown into chaos.
8.13: Then Gideon son of Joash made his way back from battle by the pass of Heres.
8.14: He seized a young man from Succoth and interrogated him. The young man wrote out the names of Succoth's leaders and elders—seventy-seven in all.
8.15: Gideon returned to the men of Succoth and said, “Here they are—Zebah and Zalmunna. You mocked me: 'Have you actually caught them yet? Why should we feed your exhausted men?' Well?”
8.16: He seized the elders of the city and, using desert thorns and briers, he gave the men of Succoth the punishment he had promised.
8.17: Then he marched to Penuel, pulled down their tower, and put the men of the city to death.
8.18: Then Gideon turned to the two captured kings: “Describe the men you killed at Tabor.” They said, “They looked exactly like you—each one carried himself like a king's son.”
8.19: He said quietly, “Those were my brothers—my own mother's sons. I swear by the LORD's life: if you had spared them, I would have spared you.”
8.20: He turned to his eldest son, Jether: “Kill them.” But Jether could not move—he was still just a boy and fear gripped him.
8.21: So the two kings taunted Gideon: “Do it yourself. A man's strength matches his manhood.” Gideon stepped forward and killed them, then stripped the golden crescent ornaments from their camels' necks.
8.22: The men of Israel came to Gideon: “Be our king—you and your sons after you. You have rescued us from Midian's grip.”
8.23: Gideon refused: “I will not be your king, and neither will any of my sons. The LORD is your king.”
8.24: But he did ask one thing: “Let every man give me a gold earring from his plunder.” (The Midianites, being Ishmaelites, wore gold earrings.)
8.25: They agreed eagerly. They spread out a cloak and each man threw in the gold earrings he had taken.
8.26: The gold earrings alone weighed seventeen hundred shekels—not counting the crescent ornaments, the pendants, the royal purple robes worn by the kings of Midian, or the golden chains around their camels' necks.
8.27: With the gold Gideon made an ephod and set it up in his hometown of Ophrah. All Israel began venerating it there as a cult object—and it became a snare that trapped Gideon himself and his whole household. The man who tore down Baal's altar had built a new idol.
8.28: So Midian was broken before Israel and never recovered. The land enjoyed forty years of peace throughout Gideon's lifetime.
8.29: Jerubbaal son of Joash retired to his own home.
8.30: Gideon had fathered seventy sons by his many wives.
8.31: His concubine in Shechem bore him another son, whom he named Abimelech—a name that would cast a long shadow.
8.32: Gideon son of Joash died at a ripe old age and was buried in the family tomb at Ophrah, in the territory of the Abiezrites.
8.33: No sooner was Gideon in the grave than Israel turned back to the Baals, making Baal-berith—the Canaanite god of covenants—their deity.
8.34: They forgot the LORD their God—the one who had rescued them from every enemy surrounding them.
8.35: Nor did they show any steadfast loyal love to Gideon's family—no gratitude for all the good he had done for Israel.

9.1: Abimelech son of Jerubbaal went to Shechem, where his mother's family lived, and met with all her relatives, saying to them:
9.2: “Say this to all the citizens of Shechem: 'Is it better to be ruled by seventy of Gideon's sons, or by one man? And remember—my mother is one of you. I am your blood.”
9.3: His mother's family passed the message along to all the leaders of Shechem, and the people's loyalties shifted toward Abimelech. “He is one of us,” they said.
9.4: From the treasury of Baal-berith—the god of covenants—they gave him seventy pieces of silver. With that money, Abimelech hired a band of desperate, reckless men as his followers. Covenant funds bought a covenant-breaker's army.
9.5: Then he went to his father's home at Ophrah and murdered seventy of his brothers—Gideon's sons—one by one on a single stone. Only the youngest, Jotham, survived, because he had hidden himself.
9.6: Then all the citizens of Shechem and the people of Beth-millo assembled and crowned Abimelech king beside the great oak at the sacred pillar in Shechem.
9.7: When Jotham heard what had been done, he climbed to the summit of Mount Gerizim and cried out in a loud voice: “Listen to me, men of Shechem—if you want God to hear you, hear me first!”
9.8:
Once upon a time the trees decided to anoint a king.
They went to the olive tree first: 'Come, be our king.'
9.9:
But the olive tree said:
'Should I give up my oil—the oil that honors gods and mortals alike—
just to go and lord it over the trees?'
9.10: So the trees turned to the fig tree: 'Come, you be our king.'
9.11:
But the fig tree said:
'Should I abandon my sweetness and my good fruit
to go and rule over the trees?'
9.12: Then the trees went to the vine: 'Come, you rule over us.'
9.13:
But the vine said:
'Should I leave my wine—the wine that gladdens both gods and mortals—
to go swaying over the other trees?'
9.14: At last all the trees turned to the thornbush: 'Come then—you be our king.'
9.15:
And the thornbush replied:
'If you really mean to anoint me your king, then come shelter under my shade!
But if you're not in earnest—let fire burst from this thornbush and consume the cedars of Lebanon.'
9.16: Now hear me out: if you acted in genuine good faith when you made Abimelech king—if you have dealt honorably with the family of Jerubbaal and given him what his service deserved—
9.17: (my father fought for you and threw his life into the balance, and he delivered you from Midian's hand—
9.18: yet today you have risen up against his family, butchered seventy of his sons on a single stone, and crowned a slave-woman's son king over Shechem—simply because he shares your blood)
9.19: —then, if your conscience is clear, enjoy your king and let him enjoy you.
9.20: But if not—if your conscience knows better—then let fire blaze out from Abimelech and burn up Shechem and Beth-millo, and let fire burst from Shechem and Beth-millo and consume Abimelech.
9.21: Then Jotham fled and ran for his life to Beer, where he stayed in hiding, afraid of his brother Abimelech.
9.22: Three years into Abimelech's reign over Israel,
9.23: God sent an evil spirit to sow discord between Abimelech and the people of Shechem. The Shechemites began to act in bad faith against him.
9.24: God's purpose: that the blood of Gideon's seventy sons would be avenged—the guilt falling on Abimelech who killed them, and on the men of Shechem who made it possible.
9.25: The men of Shechem planted ambushes on the hilltops and began raiding every caravan that passed. Word reached Abimelech.
9.26: Then Gaal son of Ebed arrived in Shechem with his clan, and the Shechemites shifted their allegiance to him.
9.27: During the harvest festival they went to their vineyards, pressed the grapes, held a celebration, went into the temple of their god—and over the wine began cursing Abimelech.
9.28: Gaal son of Ebed called out: “Who is this Abimelech, and why does Shechem bow to him? He is just the son of Jerubbaal—a mercenary king—and Zebul is nothing but his flunkey! We are the true sons of Shechem, the sons of Hamor! Why should we serve this man?”
9.29: If only this people were under my command—I would get rid of Abimelech! I would tell him: 'Bring all your men and come out and fight!'
9.30: Zebul, Abimelech's governor in the city, heard what Gaal had said and his anger flared.
9.31: He sent secret word to Abimelech: “Gaal son of Ebed has come to Shechem with his clan and is turning the whole city against you.
9.32: March tonight under cover of darkness—you and your men—and set an ambush in the fields.
9.33: At first light, attack the city. When Gaal and his men come out to meet you, do what needs to be done.“
9.34: Abimelech moved that night with all his men, and they surrounded Shechem in four assault groups.
9.35: Gaal son of Ebed went out and took his position at the city gate—just as Abimelech and his forces rose from their hiding places.
9.36: Gaal looked up and said to Zebul, “Look—people are coming down from the hilltops!” Zebul said, “You're just seeing shadows on the hillside.”
9.37: Gaal looked again: “No—there's a column coming down from the center of the land, and another coming by the Diviners' Oak.”
9.38: Then Zebul turned on him: “Now where are all your brave words? 'Who is Abimelech that we should serve him?' Well? There is the people you sneered at. Go out and fight them!”
9.39: So Gaal led the Shechemites out to face Abimelech.
9.40: Abimelech drove him back—Gaal fled, and his men fell wounded in heaps all the way to the city gate.
9.41: Abimelech pulled back to Arumah; Zebul expelled Gaal and his clan from Shechem permanently.
9.42: The next day the Shechemites went out to work their fields. Word came to Abimelech.
9.43: He split his troops into three groups, set an ambush in the fields, and watched. When the Shechemites came out, he fell on them.
9.44: Abimelech's group charged the gate and held it, while the other two companies swept through the fields, killing everyone they found.
9.45: Abimelech fought until the city fell. He killed its people, tore down every building, and salted the ruins—a cursing ritual, consecrating the ground to desolation.
9.46: When the people of the Tower of Shechem heard what had happened, they retreated into the fortified temple of El-berith.
9.47: Abimelech was told that they had all taken refuge there.
9.48: Abimelech led his men up Mount Zalmon. He took an axe, cut a great bundle of brushwood, hoisted it onto his shoulder, and ordered his men: “Quickly—do exactly what I've done.”
9.49: Every soldier cut his bundle and they piled the wood against the stronghold and set it ablaze. About a thousand men and women died in the fire—the entire population of the Tower of Shechem. Jotham's curse had begun its work.
9.50: From there Abimelech marched to Thebez, besieged it, and took the town.
9.51: But inside the town stood a strong tower. The whole population—men, women, and leaders alike—took refuge in it, locked the door, and climbed to the roof.
9.52: Abimelech pressed his attack on the tower, approaching the door to burn it down.
9.53: Then a woman dropped the upper stone of a handmill from the roof onto Abimelech's head and shattered his skull.
9.54: He gasped to his armor-bearer, “Draw your sword and finish me—don't let them say a woman killed me.” So the young man drove his sword through him, and he died. He got what he demanded, though not what he deserved.
9.55: When Israel saw that Abimelech was dead, they all went home.
9.56: So God settled the account of Abimelech for murdering his seventy brothers.
9.57: And the men of Shechem received their own reckoning. Jotham's curse from the top of Mount Gerizim had come to pass, word for word.

10.1: After Abimelech, Tola son of Puah son of Dodo—a man of Issachar—arose to deliver Israel. He made his home in Shamir in the Ephraimite highlands.
10.2: He governed Israel for twenty-three years, then died and was buried in Shamir.
10.3: After him came Jair the Gileadite, who governed Israel for twenty-two years.
10.4: He had thirty sons who each rode a donkey and governed thirty towns in Gilead—a cluster of settlements still called Havvoth-jair to this day.
10.5: Jair died and was buried at Kamon.
10.6: Once again Israel did what was evil in the LORD's sight. They served the Baals and the Ashtoreths—and on top of that, the gods of Syria, Sidon, Moab, Ammon, and Philistia. They abandoned the LORD entirely and stopped serving him.
10.7: The LORD's anger blazed against Israel and he sold them—delivered them as property—into the hands of the Philistines and the Ammonites.
10.8: That year they crushed and ground down Israel for eighteen long years—particularly those living east of the Jordan in the former Amorite territory of Gilead.
10.9: The Ammonites even crossed the Jordan to wage war against Judah, Benjamin, and Ephraim. Israel was in utter distress.
10.10: Israel cried out to the LORD: “We have sinned against you. We abandoned our God and served the Baals.”
10.11: The LORD answered: “Was it not I who rescued you from Egypt? From the Amorites? From the Ammonites and the Philistines?
10.12: The Sidonians, the Amalekites, the Maonites—they all oppressed you, and every time you cried out I delivered you.
10.13: Yet you have abandoned me and gone after other gods. I will not rescue you again.
10.14: Go and cry to the gods you chose. Let them deliver you in your hour of need.“
10.15: Israel said to the LORD, “We have sinned. Do with us whatever you think right. Only please—save us today.”
10.16: They removed every foreign god from their midst and began serving the LORD again—and he could bear their suffering no longer. His aching heart gave way.
10.17: The Ammonites mobilized and camped in Gilead. Israel gathered and made camp at Mizpah.
10.18: The leaders of Gilead said among themselves, “Which man will step forward and lead the attack against Ammon? Whoever does it shall be ruler over all of Gilead.”

11.1: Jephthah the Gileadite was a champion of valor—but he was born of a prostitute. Gilead was his father.
11.2: Gilead's legitimate wife also bore him sons. When they grew up, they drove Jephthah out: “You get nothing from our father's estate—you're the son of another woman.”
11.3: So Jephthah ran away to the land of Tob. There he attracted a band of desperate men around him and they lived by raiding.
11.4: In the course of time, the Ammonites launched a war against Israel.
11.5: When the Ammonites attacked, the elders of Gilead went to Tob to bring Jephthah back.
11.6: They said to him, “Come back. Lead us against the Ammonites.”
11.7: Jephthah said to them, “Didn't you hate me and throw me out of my father's house? Now that you're in trouble, you come to me?”
11.8: The elders said, “Precisely why we have come. Lead us against Ammon, and you will be ruler over all of Gilead.”
11.9: Jephthah said, “If you bring me home to fight Ammon—and the LORD delivers them to me—then I will be your ruler.”
11.10: The elders replied, “The LORD himself is our witness. We will do exactly as you say.”
11.11: Jephthah went with the elders, and the people appointed him head and commander. He laid out all the terms before the LORD at Mizpah—making the agreement a covenant before God.
11.12: Jephthah opened diplomatic talks, sending a message to the Ammonite king: “What grievance do you have with us that you have invaded our land?”
11.13: The Ammonite king replied: “When Israel came up from Egypt, they seized our land—from the Arnon all the way to the Jabbok and the Jordan. Give it back, peaceably.”
11.14: Jephthah sent back his own message:
11.15: “Jephthah says this: Israel took neither Moabite nor Ammonite land.
11.16: When Israel left Egypt, they passed through the wilderness to the Red Sea and arrived at Kadesh.
11.17: Israel asked Edom for passage—refused. They asked Moab—also refused. So Israel stayed at Kadesh.
11.18: So Israel went the long way around, skirting Edom and Moab, coming up along Moab's east side, and camping beyond the Arnon River—never setting foot inside Moab's territory, because the Arnon was Moab's border.
11.19: Then Israel sent messengers to Sihon king of the Amorites in Heshbon: 'Let us pass through your land to reach our destination.'
11.20: But Sihon refused to trust Israel with passage through his land. He mustered his entire army, camped at Jahaz, and attacked Israel.
11.21: The LORD, the God of Israel, handed Sihon and his whole army over to Israel. Israel defeated them and occupied all the Amorite territory.
11.22: The whole region from the Arnon to the Jabbok, from the desert to the Jordan—they occupied it all.
11.23: The LORD, the God of Israel, drove out the Amorites—so now you want to take their land from us?
11.24: You hold what your god Chemosh gives you. We hold what the LORD our God has given us—and that is the principle that settles this.
11.25: Are you any greater than Balak son of Zippor, king of Moab? Did he wage war against Israel? Did he challenge us in court?
11.26: Israel has held Heshbon and Aroer and all the towns along the Arnon for three hundred years. If this land were really yours, why didn't you reclaim it in three centuries?
11.27: I have done you no wrong. You are the one committing injustice by attacking us. Let the LORD, who is the Judge, render his verdict today between Israel and Ammon.“
11.28: The Ammonite king ignored every word of it.
11.29: Then the Spirit of the LORD came upon Jephthah. He swept through Gilead and Manasseh, through Mizpah of Gilead, and crossed over to face the Ammonites.
11.30: Before the battle, Jephthah made a vow to the LORD: “If you truly deliver the Ammonites into my hands,
11.31: then whatever comes through my front door to greet me when I return victorious from Ammon—it will be the LORD's, and I will offer it as a burnt offering.“ The words were rash, and the text holds its breath.
11.32: Jephthah crossed to engage the Ammonites—and the LORD gave them into his hands.
11.33: He devastated them from Aroer to Minnith—twenty towns—all the way to Abel-keramim, a crushing defeat. Ammon was broken before Israel.
11.34: Jephthah came home to Mizpah—and his daughter came out dancing to meet him, tambourine in hand. She was his one and only child. He had no other.
11.35: When he saw her, he tore his robes in grief and cried out, “Oh, my daughter—you have broken me! You are the one who has ruined me. I made a vow to the LORD and I cannot take it back.”
11.36: She said to him, “Father, you gave your word to the LORD. Do what you vowed—because the LORD has vindicated you against your enemies, the Ammonites.”
11.37: But she made one request: “Give me two months. Let me go to the mountains with my companions and mourn that I will die a virgin.”
11.38: He said, “Go.” He sent her away for two months, and she went into the mountains with her friends and mourned that she would never know a husband.
11.39: At the end of two months she came home to her father, and he fulfilled his vow. She had never known a man. And from this, a tradition arose in Israel—
11.40: —the daughters of Israel would go out every year for four days to commemorate the daughter of Jephthah the Gileadite.

12.1: The Ephraimites mobilized and came north, confronting Jephthah: “You fought Ammon without us? We'll burn your house down over your head!”
12.2: Jephthah answered: “My people and I were in a desperate fight with Ammon. I called on you—you never came.
12.3: When I saw no help was coming, I risked my life and crossed over, and the LORD handed them to me. So why are you here now, to make war against me?“
12.4: Jephthah—unlike Gideon before him—chose force over diplomacy. He gathered the Gileadites and attacked Ephraim. The Ephraimites had heaped insult on injury, calling the Gileadites nothing but Ephraimite deserters; now they paid for it.
12.5: The Gileadites seized the Jordan fords before the fleeing Ephraimites could reach them. When a fugitive arrived and asked to cross, they would ask: “Are you an Ephraimite?” If he said no—
12.6: they told him, “Say 'Shibboleth.'” But the Ephraimite dialect could not produce the 'sh' sound—they said 'Sibboleth.' The difference was a death sentence. Forty-two thousand Ephraimites were killed at the Jordan fords. A shared language had become a weapon.
12.7: Jephthah governed Israel for six years. Then he died and was buried in a town of Gilead.
12.8: After him, Ibzan of Bethlehem governed Israel.
12.9: He had thirty sons and thirty daughters. He arranged marriages for all of them—his daughters out and other clans' daughters in for his sons—weaving a wide network of alliances. He governed Israel seven years.
12.10: Then Ibzan died and was buried in Bethlehem.
12.11: After him, Elon the Zebulunite governed Israel for ten years.
12.12: Elon the Zebulunite died and was buried at Aijalon in the territory of Zebulun.
12.13: After him, Abdon son of Hillel the Pirathonite governed Israel.
12.14: He had forty sons and thirty grandsons—seventy young men riding donkeys. He governed Israel eight years.
12.15: Abdon son of Hillel the Pirathonite died and was buried at Pirathon in Ephraim, in the hill country of the Amalekites.

13.1: Israel fell back into evil—again—in the LORD's sight. And the LORD handed them over to the Philistines for forty years.
13.2: In the town of Zorah there lived a man named Manoah, from the tribe of Dan. His wife was barren—she had never conceived.
13.3: The angel of the LORD appeared to the woman and said: 'You are barren—you have never conceived—but you will conceive and give birth to a son.'
13.4: From this moment: drink no wine, no beer, eat nothing ritually unclean.
13.5: You will conceive and give birth to a son. No razor must ever touch his head, because from the womb this child will be set apart to God as a Nazirite. He will be the one who begins to rescue Israel from the Philistines.
13.6: The woman hurried to tell her husband: 'A man of God came to me. His face was like the face of an angel—terrifying in its majesty. I didn't think to ask where he came from, and he didn't tell me his name.'
13.7: But he told me: 'You will conceive and bear a son. From now on drink no wine, no beer, eat nothing unclean—because this child will be a Nazirite to God from the moment he is conceived until the day he dies.'
13.8: Manoah prayed to the LORD: 'Lord, please—send the man of God back to us. Let him teach us how to raise the child who is coming.'
13.9: God heard Manoah's prayer. The angel of God returned to the woman while she was sitting alone in the field—Manoah was not with her.
13.10: She ran to find Manoah and said, 'He's back—the man who appeared to me before!'
13.11: Manoah got up and followed his wife to the man and asked, 'Are you the one who spoke to my wife?' 'I am,' he said.
13.12: Manoah asked, 'When your word comes true—what are the rules for raising this child? How should we rear him?'
13.13: The angel of the LORD answered Manoah: 'Everything I said to the woman, she must observe.'
13.14: She must eat nothing from the vine, drink no wine or beer, and eat nothing ritually unclean. Let her keep every one of my commands.
13.15: Manoah said to the angel of the LORD, 'Let us prepare a young goat for you—please, stay.'
13.16: 'Even if you kept me here, I would not eat your food,' the angel replied. 'But if you want to make a burnt offering, offer it to the LORD.' Manoah did not yet realize he was speaking with the angel of the LORD.
13.17: Manoah asked the angel, 'What is your name? When your word comes true we want to honor you.'
13.18: The angel answered: 'Why ask my name? It is Wonderful—beyond all telling.' The word is the same root as the name in Isaiah 9:6: Wonderful Counselor. The angel keeps his name hidden, but the word he uses is itself a title.
13.19: Manoah took the young goat and the grain offering and laid them on the rock as a sacrifice to the LORD. And the angel did something wondrous—as Manoah and his wife looked on.
13.20: As the flame leapt upward from the altar toward heaven, the angel of the LORD rose with it—ascending in the fire. Manoah and his wife fell with their faces to the ground.
13.21: The angel of the LORD never appeared to them again. And Manoah finally understood: he had been the angel of the LORD.
13.22: Manoah was terrified: 'We are going to die—we have seen God!'
13.23: His wife steadied him: 'Think. If the LORD wanted to kill us, he would not have accepted our offering, nor shown us all this—and he certainly would not have spoken this promise to us.'
13.24: The woman gave birth to a son and named him Samson. He grew, and the LORD was with him.
13.25: The Spirit of the LORD began to stir in him at intervals—at the camp of Dan between Zorah and Eshtaol. A flame was being kindled.

14.1: Samson went down to Timnah and saw a Philistine woman living there.
14.2: He came home and told his parents, 'I saw a Philistine woman in Timnah. Get her for me—I want to marry her.'
14.3: His parents objected: 'Isn't there a woman among our own people—your own kin—that you have to marry a Philistine? The uncircumcised!' But Samson answered his father: 'Get her. She is the one I want.'
14.4: His parents did not know that this was the LORD's doing—God was using Samson's desire as a pretext to move against the Philistines, who at that time ruled over Israel.
14.5: Samson traveled with his parents toward Timnah. As he came into the Timnah vineyards, a full-grown young lion suddenly roared toward him.
14.6: The Spirit of the LORD rushed upon him with power, and he tore the lion apart with his bare hands as if it were a young goat. He had no weapon. He told neither his father nor his mother what had happened.
14.7: He went down and spoke with the woman—and she pleased him.
14.8: Later, when he went back to marry the woman, he detoured to look at the lion's carcass—and found a swarm of bees inside it, and honey.
14.9: He scooped the honey out and ate it as he walked, and brought some to his parents. They ate too—but he said nothing about where it came from. He had touched a corpse, a violation of his Nazirite vow, and he kept that hidden.
14.10: His father went to the woman's family to arrange the marriage, and Samson prepared a seven-day feast—the customary wedding celebration.
14.11: When the local Philistines saw him, they brought thirty young men to be his wedding companions.
14.12: Samson said to them, 'Let me give you a riddle. Solve it within the seven days of the feast, and I'll give you thirty fine shirts and thirty sets of clothes.'
14.13: But if you cannot solve it, you owe me thirty shirts and thirty sets of clothes.' They said, 'Let's hear it.'
14.15: On the seventh day they turned to Samson's wife with a threat: 'Get the answer from your husband, or we will burn you and your father's house. Did you invite us to your wedding to strip us of our money?'
14.16: Samson's wife wept in front of him: 'You hate me. If you loved me, you'd tell me.' She kept at it. 'You gave my people a riddle you haven't shared with me!' He said, 'I haven't told my own parents—why would I tell you?'
14.17: She wept the entire seven days. He held out until the seventh day—then he cracked under her pressure and told her. She immediately passed the answer to her people.
14.18: Before the sun set on the seventh day, the men answered: 'What is sweeter than honey? What is stronger than a lion?' Samson said bitterly: 'If you had not plowed with my heifer, you never would have cracked my riddle.'
14.19: The Spirit of the LORD rushed upon him. He went to Ashkelon, killed thirty men, stripped their clothing, and brought the garments back to pay the debt. His anger blazing, he went home to his father—without his bride.
14.20: While he was gone, Samson's wife was given to the man who had been his best man.

15.1: Some time later, during wheat harvest, Samson went to visit his wife, bringing a young goat as a gift. 'I'm going to my wife's room,' he said. But her father blocked the way.
15.2: Her father said, 'I was certain you hated her—so I gave her to your companion. Her younger sister is even prettier. Take her.'
15.3: 'Whatever I do to the Philistines now, I'm in the right,' Samson said. 'They've given me every reason.'
15.4: Samson caught three hundred foxes, paired them tail to tail, and fixed a burning torch between each pair.
15.5: He set the torches ablaze and released the foxes into the Philistine fields. Fire swept through everything: the stacked sheaves, the standing crops, the vineyards, the olive trees.
15.6: When the Philistines learned who had done it and why, they came and burned the woman and her father. The woman caught in the middle of a feud paid the price.
15.7: 'Since you did this,' Samson said, 'I will not stop until I have had my revenge—and then I'm done.'
15.8: He attacked the Philistines with savage force—hip and thigh, a massive slaughter. Then he withdrew and settled in the cave at the rock of Etam.
15.9: The Philistines responded by invading Judah and deploying their forces at Lehi.
15.10: The men of Judah asked, 'Why have you invaded us?' They answered, 'We've come for Samson—to give him what he gave us.'
15.11: Three thousand men of Judah went down to the rock of Etam. 'Don't you know the Philistines are our rulers?' they said. 'What have you done to us?' Samson answered: 'I gave them exactly what they gave me.'
15.12: 'We're here to tie you up and hand you to the Philistines,' they said. Samson said, 'Swear to me first—swear you won't attack me yourselves.'
15.13: 'We will tie you and hand you over—but we will not kill you,' they promised. They bound him with two new ropes and brought him up from the rock.
15.14: When they brought him to Lehi, the Philistines shouted in triumph. At that moment the Spirit of the LORD came upon him with power. The ropes on his arms burned away like flax touched by flame, and the bonds fell from his hands.
15.15: He picked up a fresh donkey's jawbone and with it killed a thousand men.
15.16:
Samson sang out:
'With a donkey's jaw—heap on heap—
with a donkey's jaw I have killed a thousand men!'
15.17: When he had finished, he threw down the jawbone. The place was named Ramath-lehi—'the height of the jawbone.'
15.18: Then terrible thirst hit him. He cried out to the LORD: 'You gave your servant this great victory—and now I'm going to die of thirst? Fall into the hands of the uncircumcised?'
15.19: God split open a hollow in the rocky ground and water gushed out. Samson drank, his strength returned, and he came back to life. He named the spring En-hakkore—'the Spring of the One Who Called'—and it is in Lehi to this day.
15.20: He governed Israel in the era of Philistine oppression for twenty years.

16.1: Samson went to Gaza and slept with a prostitute there.
16.2: Word spread through Gaza: 'Samson is here.' They surrounded the city gate and lay in ambush all night, waiting. 'At daybreak,' they said, 'we'll kill him.'
16.3: Samson slept until midnight. Then he got up, seized the city gates—doors, posts, bar and all—hoisted them on his shoulders, and carried them to the top of the hill overlooking Hebron.
16.4: After this Samson fell in love with a woman in the Valley of Sorek. Her name was Delilah.
16.5: The Philistine warlords came to Delilah with a proposition: 'Seduce him. Find out the secret of his strength—how we can capture and subdue him. Each one of us will pay you eleven hundred pieces of silver.' Five lords: five thousand five hundred pieces in all.
16.6: So Delilah asked Samson, 'Tell me—where does your great strength come from? What would it take to overpower you?'
16.7: Samson said, 'If someone ties me with seven fresh bowstrings that haven't dried out, I'd be as weak as any man.'
16.8: The Philistine lords brought her seven fresh bowstrings, and she tied him up.
16.9: Men were hiding in the room with her. 'Samson! The Philistines!' she cried. He snapped the bowstrings like a thread of tow breaking in fire. His secret was still safe.
16.10: Delilah said to him, 'You lied to me. You made a fool of me. Tell me the real answer.'
16.11: He said, 'New ropes—ones that have never been used on anything. Tie me with those, and I'd be helpless.'
16.12: Delilah got new ropes and tied him again. 'Samson! The Philistines!' Again the hidden men were there. He snapped the ropes from his arms like thread.
16.13: 'You keep lying to me,' Delilah said. 'Tell me the truth.' He said, 'Weave the seven braids of my hair into the loom—'
16.14: She pinned the loom and called out, 'Samson! The Philistines!' He woke and pulled the whole thing loose—the pin, the weaving, everything.
16.15: 'How can you say you love me when you won't trust me?' she said. 'Three times now you've mocked me. Three times you've refused to tell me the truth.'
16.16: She pressed him day after day, never letting up, until he was exhausted to the point of death.
16.17: He finally told her everything. 'A razor has never touched my head,' he said. 'I have been a Nazirite to God since before I was born. If my hair is shaved off, my strength will leave me—I'll be like anyone else.' He had given away the one secret that mattered.
16.18: Delilah saw that he had finally told the truth. She sent word to the Philistine lords: 'Come now—this time he's told me everything.' The lords came up, bringing the silver payment.
16.19: She lulled him to sleep in her lap and summoned a man to shave his head. Seven braids gone. She began to torment him—and his strength drained away.
16.20: 'Samson! The Philistines!' He woke and thought, 'I'll do what I always do—shake them off.' He did not know that the LORD had left him.
16.21: The Philistines seized him, put out his eyes, and brought him down to Gaza in bronze chains. He spent his days grinding grain in the prison—a champion reduced to a slave.
16.22: In the darkness of the prison, his hair began to grow.
16.23: The Philistine warlords assembled for a great sacrifice to Dagon, their god, and to celebrate. 'Our god has given us Samson—our enemy—into our power!' they cried.
16.24: The crowd cheered when they saw Samson. They praised Dagon: 'Our god has given us the one who devastated our land and slaughtered our people!'
16.25: When the crowd was drunk with celebration, they called for Samson: 'Bring out the blind man—let him entertain us!' They brought him from the prison and made him perform. They stood him between the pillars.
16.26: Samson said to the boy leading him by the hand, 'Let me lean against the pillars—let me feel them.'
16.27: The great hall was packed—all the Philistine warlords present. Three thousand more men and women watched from the roof above.
16.28: Samson prayed to the LORD: 'O Lord GOD, remember me. Give me strength one more time—just this once, God. Let me have my revenge on the Philistines for the loss of my eyes.'
16.29: Samson found the two central pillars and pressed his hands against them—right hand on one, left hand on the other.
16.30: Samson said, 'Let me die with the Philistines.' He pushed with everything he had. The pillars buckled. The roof came down on the lords, on the crowd, on everyone in the building. The dead he killed in his death outnumbered all he had killed in his life.
16.31: His brothers and his father's family came down and brought his body home, burying him between Zorah and Eshtaol in the tomb of his father Manoah. He had governed Israel for twenty years.

17.1: In the hill country of Ephraim there lived a man named Micah.
17.2: He said to his mother, 'The eleven hundred pieces of silver that were stolen from you—the curse you spoke in my hearing—I took it. It's here.' His mother said, 'Bless you, my son—the LORD bless you.'
17.3: When he returned the silver, his mother said, 'I had already dedicated this silver to the LORD—for my son—to make a carved idol and a cast image.' She gave it back to him.
17.4: His mother took two hundred of the silver pieces and gave them to a craftsman, who made a carved image and a cast idol. These were installed in Micah's house.
17.5: Micah set up a complete household shrine—an ephod, household divination idols (teraphim)—and ordained one of his own sons as the family priest.
17.6: In those days Israel had no king—and every person did whatever seemed right to themselves.
17.7: A young man from Bethlehem in Judah—a Levite who had been living there—was looking for a place.
17.8: The Levite left Bethlehem in search of a place to settle. His travels brought him to the hill country of Ephraim and to the house of Micah.
17.9: Micah asked, 'Where are you from?' 'I'm a Levite from Bethlehem in Judah,' he said, 'looking for a place to live.'
17.10: Micah said, 'Live with me. Be my father and my priest. I'll pay you ten silver a year, provide your clothing and food.' The Levite agreed.
17.11: The Levite was content. Micah treated him like one of his own sons.
17.12: Micah formally ordained the Levite, who became the household priest living in Micah's shrine.
17.13: Micah said with satisfaction, 'Now I know the LORD will bless me—I have a real Levite as my priest.' He did not notice the contradiction: invoking the LORD's blessing upon a shrine built on stolen silver, filled with carved idols.

18.1: In those days Israel had no king. The tribe of Dan was still without a settled territory—no portion had fallen to them among the tribes—and they were looking for land to call their own.
18.2: The Danites sent five warriors from their clans—from Zorah and Eshtaol—to scout out land for them to settle. 'Go reconnoiter the land,' they were told. When the scouts reached the Ephraimite highlands they spent the night at Micah's house.
18.3: They recognized the young Levite's voice. They went in and said, 'What are you doing here? Who brought you? What's your arrangement?'
18.4: 'Micah hired me,' he said. 'I serve as his household priest.'
18.5: 'Ask God for us,' they said. 'We need to know—will this mission succeed?'
18.6: 'Go in peace,' the priest said. 'The LORD is with your way.' He spoke with confidence he had no grounds for—an idol-priest pronouncing the divine name.
18.7: The scouts came to Laish and found a people living without a care—like the Sidonians in their ways, quiet and secure, with no strong ruler and no ally nearby. They were far from Sidon, isolated, defenseless.
18.8: They returned to their kinsmen at Zorah and Eshtaol. 'What did you find?' their people asked.
18.9: 'Get up—let's go! We've seen the land and it's excellent. What are you waiting for? Don't just sit there. Go in and take it.'
18.10: 'You'll find a people at ease, wide-open land, and God has given it into your hands—a place that lacks nothing this earth produces.'
18.11: Six hundred armed Danite warriors set out from Zorah and Eshtaol.
18.12: They camped at Kiriath-jearim in Judah—the place still called Mahaneh-dan, 'the Camp of Dan,' to the west of Kiriath-jearim.
18.13: From there they moved on into the Ephraimite highlands and arrived at the house of Micah.
18.14: The five scouts spoke up: 'You know there's a shrine in that house—an ephod, household idols, a carved image, a cast idol. Think about what you want to do with that.'
18.15: They turned toward the house and went to the young Levite at Micah's shrine and greeted him.
18.16: While they talked, the six hundred armed Danites stood guard at the gate.
18.17: The five scouts went in and took the carved idol, the ephod, the teraphim, and the cast image. The Levite priest stood at the gate while the six hundred soldiers held the entrance.
18.18: They went into Micah's shrine and carried everything out. The priest said, 'What are you doing?'
18.19: 'Be quiet,' they said. 'Put your hand over your mouth and come with us. Is it better to be a priest for one household, or priest to an entire tribe of Israel?'
18.20: The priest's heart leapt at the offer. He took the ephod, the teraphim, and the carved image and fell in with the people.
18.21: They turned back with their plunder—women and children and cattle and goods placed in front, the armed men forming the rear guard.
18.22: When they were well past Micah's house, his neighbors rallied and caught up with the Danite column.
18.23: They called after the Danites. The column halted. 'What's your problem, Micah?' they said. 'Why the mob?'
18.24: 'You took my gods—the gods I made—and my priest,' Micah said, 'and you walk away. What do I have left? How can you ask what's wrong with me?'
18.25: 'Keep your mouth shut,' the Danites said. 'There are men here with short tempers. Keep pushing and you'll lose your life—and your family with you.'
18.26: The Danites marched on. Micah stood and looked at the column. They were too many. He turned around and went home.
18.27: Carrying Micah's idols and his priest, the Danites came to Laish—a people living at peace, with no warning and no defense. They put them to the sword and burned the city to the ground.
18.28: There was no rescuer—the city was far from Sidon, isolated and without ally. It lay in the valley near Beth-rehob. The Danites rebuilt it and settled in their new home.
18.29: They named the city Dan, after their ancestor Dan, son of Israel—though it had been called Laish before. The northernmost city of Israel now bore the tribal name, acquired through violence.
18.30: The Danites installed Micah's carved idol. Jonathan son of Gershom served as priest—a descendant of Moses (Hebrew scribes suspended a letter to write 'Manasseh' where the original name was 'Moses,' to spare the lawgiver's name the shame of a grandson who became an idol-priest). His sons continued the priesthood until the day of exile.
18.31: They maintained that unauthorized idol throughout all the years the legitimate sanctuary stood at Shiloh—the true house of God and this false one coexisting in an era when everyone did what was right in their own eyes.

19.1: In those days when Israel had no king, a Levite made his home in the far reaches of the Ephraimite highlands. He had taken a concubine from Bethlehem in Judah.
19.2: But his concubine broke faith with him and left, making her way back to her father's house in Bethlehem, where she remained four months.
19.3: Her husband went after her, hoping to win her back with gentle words. He took his servant and two donkeys. She brought him inside her father's house, and her father was genuinely glad to welcome him.
19.4: His father-in-law pressed him to stay, and the days slipped by — three days of eating and drinking in the house.
19.5: On the fourth morning he rose early to leave. His father-in-law said, “Have something to eat first — then go.”
19.6: They sat and ate together. The father pressed again: “Stay the night — rest, enjoy yourself.”
19.7: The man got up to go, but his father-in-law kept pressing, and he stayed another night.
19.8: He rose early on the fifth morning to go. Again his father-in-law said, “Eat something first.” They lingered until the afternoon.
19.9: When the man finally rose to leave — he, his concubine, and his servant — his father-in-law made one last appeal: “The day is nearly spent. Stay one more night. Rise early tomorrow and go home.” Four days of delay had already cost them.
19.10: But this time he refused to stay. He set out with his concubine and his two saddled donkeys and traveled as far as Jebus — the city that is Jerusalem.
19.11: As they passed near Jebus the day was almost gone. The servant said, “Let's stop here — spend the night in this city.”
19.12: His master said, “No — I won't sleep in a foreign city. We push on to Gibeah.” A fatal choice.
19.13: He told his servant, “We'll stop at one of those Israelite towns — Gibeah or Ramah — and bed down for the night.”
19.14: They pressed on, and the sun set as they arrived at Gibeah, a town belonging to Benjamin.
19.15: They turned in to spend the night at Gibeah. The Levite sat in the town square — and no one came out to offer them shelter. Not one person.
19.16: At evening an old man came in from the fields. He was from the Ephraimite highlands — a sojourner in Gibeah, not a native — for the townspeople were Benjamites.
19.17: He looked up and saw the traveler sitting in the square. “Where are you headed?” he asked. “Where do you come from?”
19.18: The Levite answered: “We're traveling from Bethlehem back to the far side of the Ephraimite highlands — that's my home. I went down to Bethlehem and now I'm heading back. But not a soul in this town has opened a door to us.”
19.19: We have everything we need — fodder for the donkeys, bread and wine for myself, my concubine, and my servant. We ask only for shelter.“
19.20: The old man said, “Peace to you — you are welcome here. Whatever you need is on me. Only, do not sleep in the open square.”
19.21: He brought them inside, fed the donkeys, and they washed the road from their feet and sat down to eat and drink.
19.22: While they were still eating and drinking, the house was surrounded. A mob of lawless men from the city hammered on the door and shouted at the old man: “Send out the man who came to your house — we want him.”
19.23: The old man went out to face them: “No, my brothers — no! Don't do this wicked thing. This man is a guest in my house. You must not commit this outrage.”
19.24: Look — I have a virgin daughter, and this man has a concubine. I will bring them out. Do with them as you will. Only do not do this shameful thing to my male guest.
19.25: The men would not listen. So the Levite seized his concubine and threw her out to them. They violated her and brutalized her all night long until dawn, then let her go.
19.26: At the first gray light she dragged herself to the door of the house where her lord was and fell there, her hands on the threshold.
19.27: In the morning her lord opened the door to leave — and there she was. His concubine, fallen at the threshold, her hands still reaching toward the door.
19.28: He said to her, “Get up. We're going.” She did not answer. He lifted her body onto the donkey and rode home.
19.29: When he reached home, he took a knife and dismembered her — bones and all — into twelve pieces, and sent one piece to each of the twelve territories of Israel.
19.30: Everyone who saw it said the same: “Nothing like this has happened in Israel since the day we came up from Egypt. Look at it. Think. Say something.”

20.1: All Israel came out as one — from Dan in the far north to Beersheba in the south, and the people of Gilead besides — and assembled before the LORD at Mizpah.
20.2: The commanders of every tribe of Israel took their places in this great assembly before God. Four hundred thousand swords were present.
20.3: Benjamin heard that all Israel had gathered at Mizpah. The assembly said, “Tell us — how did this atrocity happen?”
20.4: The Levite — the murdered woman's husband — stood and testified: “My concubine and I came to Gibeah in Benjamin to spend the night.
20.5: The men of Gibeah surrounded the house that night to kill me. They seized my concubine instead and abused her until she died.
20.6: I cut her body in pieces and sent it across every part of Israel, because what was done in Gibeah is a covenant-shattering outrage — and all Israel must judge it.
20.7: You are all Israel. This moment belongs to you — speak, decide, act.
20.8: Every man rose as one. “None of us goes home. Not one of us returns to his tent.”
20.9: This is what we do to Gibeah: we march on it, and we decide the order by lot.
20.10: We will conscript one in ten to supply the army, so that when the rest arrive at Gibeah they can deal with its crime as it deserves.
20.11: All Israel's fighting men gathered against Gibeah, bound together like a single fist.
20.12: But first the tribes sent envoys through all of Benjamin: “What is this evil that has been done among you?”
20.13: Surrender the guilty men of Gibeah so we can execute them and purge this evil from Israel.“ But Benjamin would not listen to their brothers.
20.14: Instead, Benjamin mustered from every city, converging on Gibeah to make war against the rest of Israel.
20.15: Benjamin's forces: twenty-six thousand swordsmen from across the tribe, plus seven hundred elite fighters from Gibeah itself.
20.16: Among them were seven hundred specialized left-handed slingers, each able to hit a target the width of a hair without missing.
20.17: Israel's forces, excluding Benjamin, numbered four hundred thousand swords — every one a trained soldier.
20.18: Before marching, Israel went to Bethel and sought God's direction: “Who leads the attack against Benjamin?” The LORD answered: “Judah goes first.”
20.19: Israel rose at dawn and deployed against Gibeah.
20.20: Israel marched out and formed up for battle before Gibeah.
20.21: Benjamin sallied out of Gibeah and cut down twenty-two thousand Israelites. A shattering defeat — God had said \“go,\” and Israel had still lost.
20.22: The men of Israel steeled themselves and returned to the same battle position as before.
20.23: They went to Bethel and wept before the LORD until evening, then asked: “Do we go back out against our brother Benjamin?” The LORD again said, “Go up against him.” Divine authorization was given — but divine guarantee was not yet.
20.24: So Israel advanced against Benjamin a second time.
20.25: Again Benjamin came out of Gibeah and cut down eighteen thousand more Israelite swordsmen. Two days. Forty thousand dead. And still no king.
20.26: Now all Israel — the whole nation — went up to Bethel. They wept before the LORD, sat fasting until evening, and offered burnt offerings and fellowship offerings. This time they came not merely for tactical advice but in genuine grief and repentance.
20.27: (In those days the ark of God's covenant was kept at Bethel — Israel could seek the LORD directly:
20.28: Phinehas son of Eleazar son of Aaron ministered before it.) They asked: “Do we attack Benjamin again, or do we stop?” This time the LORD answered with a promise: “Go up. Tomorrow I will hand them over to you.”
20.29: Israel placed hidden forces all around Gibeah.
20.30: On the third day Israel marched against Benjamin with the same front as before — but this time the ambush lay waiting.
20.31: Benjamin came out and was drawn away from the city, just as planned. They began killing Israelites as before — about thirty men — along the roads toward Bethel and in the open field.
20.32: Benjamin thought, “We're winning again!” But Israel's retreat was deliberate — they were pulling Benjamin away from the city and onto open ground.
20.33: At Baal-tamar Israel wheeled and formed their line — and the hidden ambush erupted from the west side of Gibeah.
20.34: Ten thousand picked fighters from all Israel crashed into Gibeah itself. The fighting was savage — and Benjamin still did not understand that the trap had shut.
20.35: The LORD struck Benjamin down before Israel. Twenty-five thousand one hundred Benjamite swordsmen died that day — the LORD's doing, not Israel's numbers.
20.36: Only now did Benjamin see that the battle was lost. Israel's withdrawal on the first two days had been a feint; the real weapon was the ambush behind them.
20.37: The ambush stormed into Gibeah, fanned out through every street, and put everyone in the city to the sword.
20.38: The agreed signal between the main army and the ambush: a great pillar of smoke rising from the burning city.
20.39: During Israel's deliberate retreat, Benjamin cut down about thirty men and thought: “We're winning again, just like before.”
20.40: Then the smoke rose — a great column from the city. Benjamin looked back. Gibeah was in flames, the fire reaching toward the sky.
20.41: Israel wheeled around. Benjamin was seized with terror — they finally understood that destruction had come.
20.42: They fled toward the desert, but the battle followed them. Those who poured out of the cities ran into the slaughter from the other side.
20.43: Israel hemmed them in, chased them down, and drove them without mercy to the east of Gibeah.
20.44: Eighteen thousand Benjamites fell — every one a warrior.
20.45: The survivors turned and fled toward the rock of Rimmon in the desert. Israel cut down five thousand more along the roads, then pressed on to Gidom and killed two thousand more.
20.46: Total Benjamite dead that day: twenty-five thousand swords. A tribe nearly erased.
20.47: Six hundred men reached the rock of Rimmon in the desert and held out there for four months. These six hundred were all that remained of Benjamin.
20.48: Israel swept back through the territory of Benjamin, killing every person in every town — men, women, children, livestock — and burning every city to ash.

21.1: At Mizpah the men of Israel had sworn a binding oath: “Not one of us will give his daughter in marriage to Benjamin.” That oath now became the problem.
21.2: The people came to Bethel and sat before God until evening, weeping aloud with bitter grief.
21.3: “LORD God of Israel — why has it come to this? Why is one of our own tribes on the edge of extinction?”
21.4: The next morning they rose early, built an altar, and offered burnt offerings and fellowship offerings to the LORD.
21.5: Then Israel asked: “Which tribe failed to come to the assembly before the LORD?” For there had been a binding oath: any tribe that did not come to Mizpah faced death.
21.6: But the Israelites relented toward their brother Benjamin: “One of our own twelve tribes has been cut off.”
21.7: “How can we find wives for the survivors? We have sworn by the LORD not to give them our daughters.” The oath that seemed righteous now produced its own catastrophe.
21.8: They checked: which tribe had not come to Mizpah? The answer: not a single person from Jabesh-gilead had appeared.
21.9: The census confirmed it — Jabesh-gilead was entirely absent.
21.10: The assembly dispatched twelve thousand warriors to Jabesh-gilead with these orders: “Kill every person — men, women, and children.”
21.11: The command: devote to destruction every male and every woman who has been with a man. Spare only the virgins.
21.12: They found four hundred young women in Jabesh-gilead who had not been with a man. They were brought to the camp at Shiloh in Canaan.
21.13: The whole assembly sent messengers to the six hundred Benjamites sheltering at the rock of Rimmon, offering peace terms.
21.14: Benjamin came back. The Jabesh-gilead women were given to them as wives — four hundred women for six hundred men. Still not enough.
21.15: The people grieved for Benjamin — because the LORD himself had torn a gap in Israel's twelve tribes.
21.16: The elders asked: “What do we do for the remaining two hundred? All the women of Benjamin are gone.”
21.17: “The Benjamite survivors must have an inheritance. A tribe of Israel cannot be erased from the earth.”
21.18: But we can't give them our daughters — the oath stands: 'Cursed is anyone who gives a wife to Benjamin.' The oath had seemed righteous; now it was a trap.
21.19: “There is a yearly festival of the LORD at Shiloh — north of Bethel, east of the Bethel-to-Shechem road, south of Lebonah.”
21.20: They told the Benjamite men: “Go and hide in the vineyards.”
21.21: Watch for the daughters of Shiloh when they come out to dance at the festival. Then come out — each man seize a woman from the dancers — and take her back to the land of Benjamin.“
21.22: When their fathers and brothers come to protest, we will tell them: 'Show them grace for our sake — we simply did not have enough women from the war. And since you didn't give your daughters to them but they were seized, technically the oath is unbroken.' It was a legal fiction, and everyone knew it.
21.23: Benjamin did as instructed. Each man seized a wife from the dancing women at Shiloh. They returned to their territory, rebuilt their shattered towns, and settled in.
21.24: Then all Israel dispersed — each man back to his tribe and his family, each one returning to his inheritance.
21.25: In those days Israel had no king — and every person lived by the law of their own heart. This is what that law produces.

1.1: After Joshua died, Israel sought the LORD's guidance: "Which tribe should lead our attack against the Canaanites?"
1.2: The LORD answered: "Let Judah go first. I have already given the land into their hand."
1.3: Judah invited the tribe of Simeon: "Come and fight alongside us in our territory, and we will do the same for yours." Simeon agreed and joined them.
1.4: Judah marched out, and the LORD gave the Canaanites and Perizzites into their hands. They killed ten thousand men at Bezek.
1.5: There they encountered Adoni-bezek and defeated him along with his Canaanite and Perizzite forces.
1.6: Adoni-bezek fled, but they caught him and cut off his thumbs and big toes.
1.7: Adoni-bezek said, "I have done this to seventy kings—made them grovel under my table for scraps. Now God has given me exactly what I dealt out." They took him to Jerusalem, where he died. Even a pagan king recognized the justice.
1.8: The men of Judah then attacked Jerusalem, captured it, put its people to the sword, and burned the city.
1.9: After that they pushed on to fight the Canaanites in the highlands, the Negeb, and the western foothills.
1.10: Judah turned against Hebron—once called Kiriath-arba—and defeated the three Anakim clans there: Sheshai, Ahiman, and Talmai.
1.11: From Hebron they advanced on Debir—formerly known as Kiriath-sepher, the City of the Scroll.
1.12: Caleb offered an incentive: "Whoever takes Kiriath-sepher gets my daughter Achsah as his wife."
1.13: Othniel son of Kenaz—Caleb's younger brother—took the city. Caleb gave him Achsah as his wife.
1.14: When Achsah arrived, she persuaded Othniel to ask her father for farmland. She got down from her donkey, and Caleb asked her, "What do you want?"
1.15: She said, "You gave me dry land in the Negeb—now give me water too." Caleb granted her both the upper and lower springs.
1.16: The Kenites—descendants of Moses' father-in-law—traveled from Jericho with the tribe of Judah into the Judean wilderness near Arad and settled among them.
1.17: Judah and Simeon together struck the Canaanites of Zephath and completely destroyed it. That is why the place was renamed Hormah—"Destruction."
1.18: Judah also took the coastal cities: Gaza, Ashkelon, and Ekron, each with its surrounding villages.
1.19: The LORD was with Judah in the highlands, and they took the hill country. But the coastal plain was beyond them—the Canaanites there had iron chariots.
1.20: Hebron was given to Caleb, as Moses had promised. He drove out the three Anakim clans from the city.
1.21: Benjamin failed to expel the Jebusites from Jerusalem. So the Jebusites lived on in the city alongside the Benjaminites—and were still there when this account was written.
1.22: The tribes of Joseph—Ephraim and Manasseh—moved against Bethel, and the LORD was with them.
1.23: They sent scouts ahead to Bethel—the old Canaanite name of the city was Luz.
1.24: The scouts spotted a man leaving the city and said to him, "Show us how to get in, and we will treat you with covenant loyalty."
1.25: He led them in. They put the city to the sword but kept their word—the informant and his whole family were spared.
1.26: The man traveled north into Hittite territory and built a new city there, naming it Luz after the old one—and that name endures to this day.
1.27: Manasseh failed to clear the major cities of the Jezreel Valley: Beth-shean, Taanach, Dor, Ibleam, Megiddo—all with their surrounding villages. The Canaanites held their ground.
1.28: When Israel grew powerful enough, they pressed the Canaanites into forced labor—but never drove them out. Compromise replaced obedience.
1.29: Ephraim left the Canaanites of Gezer in place. They lived on, mixed in with Israel.
1.30: Zebulun left Kitron and Nahalol unconquered. The Canaanites stayed on and eventually became forced laborers.
1.31: Asher failed completely along the northern coast: Acco, Sidon, Ahlab, Achzib, Helbah, Aphik, Rehob—all left in Canaanite hands.
1.32: Instead of Asher possessing the land, the Canaanites swallowed Asher. The tribe never drove them out.
1.33: Naphtali likewise left Beth-shemesh and Beth-anath unconquered and settled among the Canaanites. In time they extracted forced labor from those cities, but never expelled them.
1.34: Dan fared worst of all—the Amorites pushed them back into the hills and refused to let them down into the valleys.
1.35: The Amorites held Mount Heres, Aijalon, and Shaalbim. Only when Ephraim's power bore down on them did they submit to labor—they were never expelled.
1.36: The Amorites held all the territory from the Scorpion Pass northward—a substantial stretch of the land Israel never took.

2.1: The angel of the LORD traveled from Gilgal to Bochim and addressed the entire assembly: "I brought you out of Egypt and into the land I swore to your ancestors. I bound myself: I will never break my covenant with you.
2.2: My terms were simple: make no treaty with the people of this land, and tear down their altars. You obeyed neither. What have you done?
2.3: Therefore I am withdrawing my commitment to clear them out. From now on they will be thorns in your flesh, and their gods will be a trap that catches you."
2.4: When the angel finished speaking, the whole assembly of Israel broke into weeping.
2.5: They named the place Bochim—"Weepers"—and offered sacrifices to the LORD there.
2.6: The narrative steps back: when Joshua dismissed the people, each tribe had gone to claim its allotted land.
2.7: As long as Joshua lived, and as long as the elders who had seen the LORD's great deeds survived, Israel served the LORD.
2.8: Joshua son of Nun, the LORD's servant, died at the age of one hundred and ten.
2.9: He was buried at Timnath-heres in the Ephraimite highlands, on the north slope of Mount Gaash.
2.10: That whole generation passed away. The generation that followed had no personal knowledge of the LORD or of what he had done for Israel. Memory failed, and with it, loyalty.
2.11: And Israel did what was evil in the LORD's sight: they worshiped the Baals.
2.12: They abandoned the LORD—the God who had brought them out of Egypt—and chased after the local gods of the surrounding peoples, bowing down to them. This provoked the LORD deeply.
2.13: They turned from the LORD and gave themselves to Baal and the Ashtoreths—the storm god and the fertility goddess of Canaan.
2.14: The LORD's anger blazed against Israel. He gave them into the hands of raiders who plundered them and sold them—as property—to the enemies pressing in from every side. They could not hold their ground.
2.15: Every campaign ended in disaster. The LORD had sworn this would happen, and now the oath ran its course. Israel was in utter ruin.
2.16: Yet the LORD raised up judges—rescuers—to deliver them from those who were crushing them.
2.17: Yet even then they refused to listen to those judges. They kept chasing other gods, abandoning the path their ancestors had walked. The judges' authority was ignored; the covenant pattern was already broken in their own generation.
2.18: Whenever the LORD raised up a judge, he was with that judge, and the people experienced rescue for as long as the judge lived. For the LORD could not bear to hear their groaning under oppression—his compassion kept overriding their faithlessness.
2.19: But each time a judge died, the people sank lower than before—deeper into idolatry, more stubborn in their ways. The cycle tightened: each apostasy worse than the last.
2.20: The LORD's anger burned against Israel. He declared: "This people has broken the covenant I gave their ancestors and refused to obey my voice.
2.21: Therefore I will no longer drive out the nations Joshua left behind when he died.
2.22: Those nations will remain as a test—to reveal whether Israel will walk in the LORD's ways as their ancestors did, or not."
2.23: So the LORD deliberately left those nations in place, without handing them to Joshua. The test was always part of the plan.

3.1: These are the nations the LORD left in the land to test Israel—specifically those Israelites who had no personal experience of the Canaanite wars.
3.2: There was a practical reason too: to train the younger generation in warfare—those who had never seen battle.
3.3: The nations left in place were: the five Philistine city-states, all the Canaanites, the Sidonians, and the Hivites of the Lebanon range—from Mount Baal-hermon to the approaches of Hamath.
3.4: Their purpose: to show whether Israel would actually keep the commandments the LORD gave through Moses—or not.
3.5: And Israel lived embedded among all six Canaanite peoples—Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites.
3.6: They intermarried freely and adopted their gods. The assimilation was total.
3.7: Israel did what was evil before the LORD. They forgot their God entirely and gave themselves to the Baals and Asherah poles.
3.8: The LORD's anger burned, and he gave Israel over to Cushan-rishathaim, king of Aram-naharaim—the land between the two great rivers. They were under his thumb for eight years.
3.9: Israel cried out to the LORD, and the LORD raised a deliverer: Othniel son of Kenaz, the same younger brother of Caleb who had taken Kiriath-sepher.
3.10: The Spirit of the LORD came upon him. He led Israel and went out to battle. The LORD gave Cushan-rishathaim into his hand, and he prevailed.
3.11: The land rested for forty years. Then Othniel died.
3.12: Israel fell back into evil before the LORD—and the LORD used Eglon king of Moab as his instrument of discipline.
3.13: Eglon allied with Ammon and Amalek, struck Israel, and seized Jericho—the city of palms.
3.14: Israel served Eglon of Moab for eighteen years.
3.15: They cried out again, and the LORD raised a deliverer: Ehud son of Gera, a Benjamite—and left-handed. The Israelites sent him to deliver their tribute payment to King Eglon of Moab.
3.16: Ehud crafted a double-edged short sword—about eighteen inches long—and strapped it on his right thigh under his clothing. A left-handed man reaching across to his right thigh would not be noticed in a security search.
3.17: He presented the tribute to Eglon. The king was enormously fat—a detail the narrator records with purpose.
3.18: After delivering the tribute, Ehud dismissed the servants who had carried it.
3.19: Ehud turned back at the carved-stone site near Gilgal and said, "I have a private message for you, Your Majesty." The king said, "Quiet!" and dismissed all his attendants.
3.20: Eglon was alone in his cool upper room. Ehud said, "I have a word from God for you." The king stood up to receive it—and Ehud's left hand moved.
3.21: Ehud drew the hidden blade with his left hand and drove it into the king's belly.
3.22: The blade went in so deep that the hilt followed; the fat sealed around it. Ehud left the sword embedded—and the body's contents began to seep out.
3.23: Ehud slipped out through the porch, pulled the doors of the upper room closed behind him, and locked them.
3.24: His servants came and found the doors locked. They assumed the king was using the privy and waited politely.
3.25: They waited until the delay became awkward. Finally they unlocked the door—and found the king dead on the floor.
3.26: While they had been waiting, Ehud was long gone—past the carved stones, all the way to Seirath.
3.27: Reaching the Ephraimite highlands, he blew the war trumpet. Israel's warriors came streaming down from the hills—Ehud at their head.
3.28: He declared, "Follow me! The LORD has given Moab into our hands!" They seized the Jordan crossing-points and sealed them—not a single Moabite got through.
3.29: At the fords they slaughtered about ten thousand Moabite warriors—every one of them a fighting man. Not one escaped.
3.30: Moab was broken under Israel's hand that day. The land enjoyed eighty years of peace.
3.31: After Ehud came Shamgar son of Anath. With nothing but a farmer's oxgoad, he killed six hundred Philistines—and saved Israel.

4.1: After Ehud's death, Israel fell back into evil before the LORD.
4.2: The LORD handed them over to Jabin, king of Canaan at Hazor—whose military commander, Sisera, operated from the fortress of Harosheth-hagoyim.
4.3: Israel cried to the LORD for help. For twenty years Sisera had crushed them with nine hundred iron-wheeled war chariots—the heavy armor of that era.
4.4: At that time Deborah—a prophetess, wife of Lappidoth—was governing Israel. She was both prophet and judge, the only one in Judges to hold both offices.
4.5: She held court under Deborah's Palm—her landmark between Ramah and Bethel in the Ephraimite hills—and the people came to her with their disputes.
4.6: She summoned Barak son of Abinoam from Kedesh-naphtali and said: "The LORD, Israel's God, has given you an order: march to Mount Tabor with ten thousand warriors from Naphtali and Zebulun."
4.7: "I will lure Sisera and his iron chariots to the Kishon River and deliver him into your hands." This was the LORD's word through her.
4.8: Barak said, "I'll go—but only if you come with me. If you won't come, neither will I."
4.9: She said, "I'll come. But know this: the glory of the victory will not fall to you. The LORD will give Sisera into the hand of a woman." She rose and went with Barak to Kedesh.
4.10: Barak mustered Zebulun and Naphtali at Kedesh. Ten thousand men marched behind him—Deborah at his side.
4.11: A narrative detail that will matter: Heber the Kenite had broken from his clan—descendants of Moses' father-in-law—and pitched his tent near the great tree at Zaanannim, close to Kedesh.
4.12: Word reached Sisera: Barak had positioned his forces on Mount Tabor.
4.13: Sisera mobilized everything: all nine hundred iron chariots and his full infantry, moving from Harosheth-hagoyim toward the Kishon.
4.14: Deborah gave the order: "Now! This is the day the LORD has given Sisera into your hand. The LORD himself goes before you!" Barak charged down from Tabor with his ten thousand.
4.15: The LORD threw Sisera's entire force into panic. His chariots—the great weapon of Canaanite power—became useless in the rout. Sisera abandoned his chariot and fled on foot.
4.16: Barak ran the chariots all the way back to Harosheth-hagoyim. The entire Canaanite army was destroyed—not one man survived.
4.17: Sisera ran—on foot now—to the tent of Jael, wife of Heber the Kenite. Heber's clan had a peace treaty with Jabin, so Sisera thought he was safe.
4.18: Jael came out to meet him. "Come in, my lord, come in—don't be afraid." He entered her tent and she covered him with a blanket.
4.19: He asked for water. She gave him milk—rich, sleep-inducing milk—and covered him again.
4.20: He ordered her: "Stand at the tent door. If anyone asks whether a man is here, say no." He had no power to give such an order—and she had no intention of obeying.
4.21: Jael picked up a tent peg and a mallet. She went softly to where he lay—dead with exhaustion and sleep. She drove the peg through his temple into the ground. He died.
4.22: Barak arrived in pursuit. Jael came out: "Come—I'll show you the man you're looking for." He entered the tent and found Sisera dead, the tent peg through his skull. Deborah's prophecy was complete.
4.23: On that day God broke the power of Jabin king of Canaan before Israel.
4.24: Israel's hand grew heavier and heavier against Jabin until he was completely destroyed.

5.1: On that day Deborah sang—and Barak son of Abinoam with her:
5.2:
When the warriors of Israel let their hair down free,
when the people rose up as volunteers—
bless the LORD!
5.3:
Hear me, you kings!
Listen, you princes!
I will sing—I myself—to the LORD;
I will make music to the LORD, the God of Israel.
5.4:
LORD, when you marched out from Seir,
when you strode out from the fields of Edom,
the earth shook, the heavens poured,
the clouds burst open with rain.
5.5:
The mountains convulsed before the LORD—
that Sinai itself trembled before the LORD, the God of Israel.
5.6:
In Shamgar's day, in Jael's day,
the main roads lay empty—
travelers crept along back paths.
5.7:
Village life had died out in Israel—utterly ceased—
until I arose, Deborah,
until I arose, a mother in Israel.
5.8:
They chose new gods—and war came to their very gates.
Not a shield, not a spear to be seen
among forty thousand in Israel.
5.9:
My heart goes out to Israel's willing leaders,
to all who volunteered among the people—
bless the LORD!
5.10:
Speak of it—you who ride sleek white donkeys,
you who sit on costly saddle-blankets,
and you who walk the road.
5.11:
At the watering troughs, beyond the archers' noise,
there they retell the LORD's righteous deeds,
the triumphs of his warriors in Israel—
then the LORD's people marched down to the gates.
5.12:
Awake, awake, Deborah!
Awake, awake, burst into song!
Rise up, Barak—lead your captives away,
son of Abinoam!
5.13:
Then the survivors marched down to the great ones;
the LORD's people came down for me against the warriors.
5.15:
Issachar's princes marched with Deborah—
as Issachar, so Barak—
rushing into the valley at his feet.
But among Reuben's clans: great debates of conscience.
5.16:
Why did you stay behind with the sheep,
listening to the piping for the flocks?
Among Reuben's clans: great debates of conscience.
5.17:
Gilead camped east of the Jordan—absent.
Dan—why did you linger with your ships?
Asher sat at the harbor, snug by his coast.
5.18:
But Zebulun—they staked their lives on the field of death.
Naphtali too, on the open heights.
5.19:
The kings of Canaan came and fought—
at Taanach, by the waters of Megiddo.
They took no silver. They got nothing.
5.20:
From heaven the stars fought;
from their courses they warred against Sisera.
5.21:
The Kishon swept them away—
that ancient river, the Kishon.
March on, my soul—march on in strength!
5.22:
Then the horses' hooves hammered—
the pounding, pounding of the war-stallions.
5.23:
"Curse Meroz!" says the angel of the LORD.
"Curse its people bitterly—
because they did not come to the LORD's aid,
to the aid of the LORD against the warriors."
5.24:
Most blessed of women is Jael—
wife of Heber the Kenite,
most blessed of women who live in tents.
5.25:
He asked for water; she brought him milk.
In a chieftain's bowl she served him curds.
5.26:
Her hand reached for the tent peg,
her right hand for the mallet—
she struck Sisera, she crushed his head,
she shattered and drove through his temple.
5.27:
Between her feet he sank, he fell, he lay.
Between her feet he sank, he fell.
Where he sank—there he fell. Dead.
5.28:
Through the window she peered—Sisera's mother—
crying through the lattice:
'Why is his chariot so late?
Why do the wheels of his chariots delay?'
5.29:
Her wisest ladies answered her;
and she answered herself:
5.30:
'Are they not dividing the plunder even now?
A girl, two girls for every man—
for Sisera: plunder of dyed cloth,
plunder of embroidered cloth,
