two embroidered pieces for the neck of the conqueror?'
5.31:
So may all your enemies perish, O LORD!
But let those who love you
be like the sun rising in full strength.
And the land rested for forty years.

6.1: Israel fell back into evil before the LORD, and the LORD gave them into Midian's hand for seven years.
6.2: Midian's grip was crushing. Israel was driven to hide—in mountain dens, caves, and fortified hollows.
6.3: Every time Israel planted crops, the Midianites, Amalekites, and eastern nomads came swarming in.
6.4: They swept through all the way to Gaza, consuming everything—every crop, every animal. They left Israel nothing.
6.5: They came in like a locust swarm—camels and tents beyond counting—and left the land stripped bare.
6.6: Israel was reduced to destitution. They cried out to the LORD.
6.7: When Israel's cry reached the LORD because of Midian,
6.8: the LORD sent a prophet who delivered this word: "Thus says the LORD, God of Israel: I brought you out of Egypt, out of the slave-house.
6.9: "I rescued you from every oppressor, cleared them from before you, and gave you their land.
6.10: "I told you: I am the LORD your God—do not fear the gods of the Amorites in whose land you now live. But you have not listened." The indictment came before the deliverer.
6.11: Then the angel of the LORD came and sat under the great tree at Ophrah—the estate of Joash the Abiezrite. His son Gideon was threshing wheat inside the winepress—hidden from Midianite raiders.
6.12: The angel of the LORD appeared to him and said, "The LORD is with you, mighty warrior."
6.13: Gideon answered, "With respect, my lord—if the LORD is with us, why is all this happening? Where are the miracles our ancestors talked about? 'The LORD brought us out of Egypt,' they said. But now the LORD has abandoned us to Midian."
6.14: The LORD turned and looked directly at him: "Go in the strength you have. Deliver Israel from Midian's hand. Am I not the one who sends you?"
6.15: Gideon said, "But Lord—how can I save Israel? My clan is the weakest in Manasseh, and I am the least person in my family."
6.16: The LORD said, "I will be with you. You will strike down Midian as if it were a single man."
6.17: Gideon said, "If I have truly found favor with you, give me a sign that it is really you speaking."
6.18: "Don't leave until I come back with an offering to set before you." The angel said, "I will wait."
6.19: Gideon went inside and prepared a young goat and a large batch of unleavened bread—the kind prepared for a guest of honor. He brought the meat in a basket and the broth in a pot to the angel under the tree.
6.20: The angel of God said, "Put the meat and the bread on that rock and pour the broth over them." Gideon did it.
6.21: The angel touched the meat and bread with the tip of his staff. Fire blazed up from the rock and consumed the offering—and then the angel of the LORD was gone.
6.22: Then Gideon understood what he had encountered. He cried out, "Alas, O Lord GOD! I have seen the angel of the LORD face to face—and no one survives that!"
6.23: But the LORD spoke: "Peace. Don't be afraid. You will not die."
6.24: Gideon built an altar there and named it Yahweh-Shalom—"The LORD is Peace." It stood at Ophrah among the Abiezrites, a lasting mark of this encounter.
6.25: That same night the LORD gave Gideon his first assignment: "Take your father's second bull—the seven-year-old—and tear down your father's altar to Baal. Cut down the Asherah pole beside it.
6.26: "Then build a proper altar to the LORD your God on top of this height—stones laid in order. Use the wood from the Asherah pole to burn the bull as an offering." The first act of calling was to dismantle the false before building the true.
6.27: Gideon obeyed—with ten servants. But he waited for darkness. He was afraid of what his family and the townspeople would do.
6.28: When morning came, the townspeople found the altar of Baal smashed, the Asherah pole felled, and a fresh bull sacrifice burning on a new altar to the LORD.
6.29: The townspeople demanded, "Who did this?" The investigation led straight to Gideon son of Joash.
6.30: The men of the town came to Joash: "Hand over your son—he must die for tearing down Baal's altar and cutting down the Asherah."
6.31: Joash turned on the crowd: "Are you going to fight Baal's battles for him? Whoever defends Baal dies before morning. If he is truly a god, he can defend himself—his altar was knocked down!"
6.32: From that day Gideon was called Jerubbaal—"Let Baal contest him"—because he had torn down the altar.
6.33: Then the Midianite coalition—Midianites, Amalekites, and all the eastern tribes—crossed the Jordan and massed in the Jezreel Valley.
6.34: Then the Spirit of the LORD wrapped itself around Gideon—clothed him like a garment. He blew the trumpet, and the men of Abiezer rallied behind him.
6.35: He sent runners through all Manasseh—they rallied. He sent to Asher, Zebulun, and Naphtali—they came up to join him.
6.36: Then Gideon said to God, "If you are truly going to deliver Israel through me, as you said—
6.37: "—let me test it: I'll lay a wool fleece on the threshing floor tonight. If the fleece is wet with dew and the ground is dry, I'll know you mean to save Israel through me."
6.38: And it was so. Next morning he squeezed the fleece—a full bowl of water wrung from it, while the ground around it was dry.
6.39: Gideon said, "Don't be angry with me—but let me ask once more. This time let the fleece be dry and the ground wet with dew."
6.40: God did it that night. The fleece lay dry; dew covered all the ground around it. Gideon's hesitation had been met with patience.
